Entity_Description: Port Moody is a municipality of 33,535 (2021 Census) in Metro Vancouver, British Columbia, sitting at the head of Burrard Inlet about 19 km east of Vancouver. Named after Colonel Richard Moody, it was the original western terminus of the Canadian Pacific Railway, welcoming the first transcontinental train in 1886. Known as the City of the Arts, it blends heritage housing in Moody Centre with hillside subdivisions on Heritage Mountain and newer mixed-use villages near the Evergreen SkyTrain line. Rocky Point Park and the Murray Street brewery district anchor its waterfront.

Short_Description: Port Moody is where the Canadian Pacific Railway first reached the Pacific in 1886, and the City of the Arts still wraps around the head of Burrard Inlet — Rocky Point Park, Brewers Row on Murray Street, and the Shoreline Trail define its waterfront life. From century-old character homes in Moody Centre to steep hillside builds in Heritage Woods and the condo villages of Suter Brook and Newport Village, this is a community of very different properties with very different needs. {COMPANY_NAME} delivers {SERVICE} across all of Port Moody with the local knowledge a {BUSINESS_TYPE} needs here: inlet moisture, forested slopes, and a mix of old and new construction. Long_Description:
Port Moody was supposed to be the terminus of a nation. When the first transcontinental Canadian Pacific Railway train rolled in on July 4, 1886, this small settlement at the head of Burrard Inlet was briefly the end of the line for all of Canada — before the rails were extended west to Vancouver. That railway heritage still shapes the city: the West Coast Express and freight lines run along the waterfront through Moody Centre, and the flat land between the tracks and the inlet holds some of the oldest housing stock in the Tri-Cities.

That history matters for {SERVICE}, because Port Moody's 33,535 residents live in three very different worlds. Down near the water, Moody Centre and Pleasantside hold early-1900s character homes and post-war bungalows, where age of construction is the defining factor for any {BUSINESS_TYPE}. Up the slopes, Heritage Mountain, Heritage Woods and College Park climb the forested hillsides above the inlet — newer builds, but on steep, tree-shaded lots where Metro Vancouver's heavy November-to-March rainfall works hardest on drainage, moss and shaded exteriors. And around the Evergreen SkyTrain stations at Moody Centre and Inlet Centre, the master-planned villages of Suter Brook and Newport Village have brought strata-governed condos and townhomes, with the coordination and access requirements strata work demands.

The setting adds its own conditions. Port Moody sits where the Salish Sea reaches its furthest inland point up Burrard Inlet, so marine moisture lingers, and the surrounding second-growth forest — the same greenbelt that makes Rocky Point Park, Old Orchard Park and the Shoreline Trail so loved — keeps many properties in shade for much of the wet season. Add the craft-brewery energy of Murray Street's Brewers Row and the arts community that earned the City of the Arts its name, and you get a place where homeowners care about how things look, not just how they function.

{COMPANY_NAME} brings {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years of experience and {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP} to every {PROPERTY_TYPE} in Port Moody. Whether the job is a heritage-era home a block off St. Johns Street, a hillside property backing onto Bert Flinn Park, or a strata building near Inlet Centre, the team arrives within {RESPONSE_TIME} and starts with {ESTIMATE_TYPE}. From Ioco Road to the Coquitlam border, {SERVICE} in Port Moody starts with a call to {PHONE}.
Local_Facts:
- Population: 33,535 (2021 Census), about 19 km east of downtown Vancouver at the head of Burrard Inlet
- Original western terminus of the Canadian Pacific Railway; the first transcontinental train arrived July 4, 1886
- Officially branded the City of the Arts, with Rocky Point Park and the Murray Street brewery district (Brewers Row) anchoring the waterfront
- Served by two Evergreen SkyTrain stations (Moody Centre and Inlet Centre, opened 2016) plus West Coast Express commuter rail
- Neighbourhoods range from heritage-era Moody Centre and Pleasantside to hillside Heritage Mountain, Heritage Woods and College Park
- Named after Colonel Richard Moody, first Lieutenant-Governor of the Colony of British Columbia
- Postal area V3H; borders Anmore (4 km), Belcarra (5 km), Coquitlam (5 km) and Port Coquitlam (6 km)
